topic: Type System - Negative Impls and AutoTraits

turns.content: Hey, I'm working on my side project this weekend. Specifically, I’m trying to understand Rust's type system around marker traits like Send and Sync. Can you explain how those work, maybe with a comparison to something I already know?

Sure! Let me start by comparing it to JavaScript interfaces or TypeScript interfaces—marker traits in Rust are somewhat analogous because they define capabilities without adding any data. For example, if you have an interface like 'Serializable' that just declares what a type can do (serialize), then Send and Sync act similarly—they’re marker traits signaling the compiler that types can be safely moved to other threads or passed by reference.
Okay, but how exactly does Rust enforce these? I mean, what’s the difference between a trait with no methods (like Send) and one with negative implementations?

Good question! Traits without any methods are often called marker traits. They’re used to mark that your type can be sent across threads or shared by reference, like 'Send' and 'Sync'. Now, sometimes you might want a trait with a negative implementation—meaning it expresses something is NOT possible. This is done using the '!' (never) type in function signatures.
So if I have a Send or Sync trait that has no methods but just implements 'Send' and 'Sync', what’s happening behind the scenes?

When you implement 'Send' or 'Sync' for your type, Rust is essentially saying “I promise this type can be safely moved between threads” or “this type can be shared by reference.” These traits are special because they’re auto-traits. That means if a struct contains only types that already implement Send and Sync, it gets them automatically without you having to write any code.
That makes sense. But what happens when I have a custom type with no methods? Can I manually add 'Send' or 'Sync'?

Yes! You can implement Send and Sync for any struct that contains only Send and Sync types, like pointers to heap data. But if your struct has fields that aren’t Send or Sync (like Rc), you’d have to manually opt out of implementing Send/Sync yourself.
So, does Rust ever need a negative implementation? Like, saying 'This type cannot be sent between threads'?

Yes! Sometimes you might want to express that something is not Send or Sync. This comes up with certain types involving raw pointers or unsafe code—Rust lets you declare this via a negative implementation, like 'impl !Send for MyUnsafeType' if it’s impossible to make it safe to move.
Ah, so marker traits and negative implementations are just ways of signaling certain guarantees in Rust. And auto-traits help propagate those signals without boilerplate?

Exactly! You got it. Marker traits like Send and Sync help the compiler reason about your types' capabilities, and by using auto-traits plus explicit negative implementations, you can precisely control what guarantees your type makes—leading to safer concurrency.

topic: Type System - Negative Impls and AutoTraits

turns.content: I'm trying to add a new trait to our open-source project but I keep getting errors about negative impls. The compiler says some types don't implement Send or Sync even though they seem like they should, and I can't figure out how to handle this.

That frustration is very common when you first dive into Rust's type system. Think of it like a security guard at an airport; Send and Sync are badges that types must carry, but some types just don't get those badges because they might leak secrets or cause panics if moved between threads. The compiler acts as the gatekeeper ensuring safety.
So I can't just add a trait that requires Send to all my types, right? Some of them are inherently unsafe?

Exactly. You cannot require Send on a type if the implementation itself contains something like an unbounded iterator or raw pointers without proper bounds. The compiler will reject your trait definition because those types don't implement it, even if you think they should.
What about auto traits? Does that help with the propagation issue I'm seeing?

Auto traits like Send and Sync are special; they propagate automatically through composition without needing explicit bounds on every inner type. However, if a type has an auto trait bound but also contains something that doesn't implement it, the whole chain breaks.
Can you give me an example of how negative reasoning works here? Like when I try to use !Send?

Negative traits are like saying 'this type must not implement Send'. You can define a trait that requires !Send, but you cannot require it on any type because no type in Rust is guaranteed to fail Send. It's a logical impossibility.
So if I need a marker trait for internal use, how do I handle the auto trait propagation?

You can create your own marker traits that don't conflict with Send or Sync. For example, you might define a trait called InternalMarker and avoid using !Send on it since no type will ever satisfy that constraint. Instead, use regular bounds like 'a: Trait + Send.
I'm still confused about why the compiler is flagging my struct as not implementing Send even though I added the necessary fields.
The compiler checks every field in your struct. If any single field fails to implement Send, the entire struct fails too. It's like a chain where one weak link breaks the whole system.
Can you clarify if I can use auto traits on my own custom marker trait without issues?

Yes, but be careful. Auto traits like Send are baked into the type system itself, so you cannot add them to your own custom traits unless those traits are actually implemented by all relevant types. If you're creating a marker trait for internal use, avoid using negative bounds.
Okay, I think I understand now. The key is avoiding !Send and relying on auto trait propagation instead of manual checks.

That's a great summary. Just remember that negative traits like !Send are intentionally forbidden because they would break the safety guarantees Rust provides. Focus on using regular bounds and letting the compiler guide you through type composition.
